\begin{document}

\begin{center}
    {\LARGE \bfseries Linear Algebra Foundations for Systems}\\[6pt]
    {\large Prepared by Staples Education}\\[4pt]
    {\itshape Unit 14: Vectors, Transformations, Determinants, and Eigentheory}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4em}

\tableofcontents
\vspace{0.4em}

\begin{topicsbox}[Unit 14 at a Glance: Linear Algebra for Systems]
Before coupled differential equations can be solved, the language of linear algebra has to be in place. A matrix is read geometrically: its columns say where the basis vectors land, its determinant says how it scales area and whether it flips orientation, and its eigenvectors mark the special directions it merely stretches. The payoff is the eigenvalue method --- guessing $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ turns the system $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ into the algebraic eigenvalue problem $A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}$.

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em, itemsep=3pt]
    \item \textbf{Vectors and transformations} --- linear combinations, span, basis, and the matrix whose \emph{columns are the images of the basis vectors}.
    \item \textbf{The determinant} --- the signed area-scaling factor $ad-bc$; zero means collapse, negative means an orientation flip.
    \item \textbf{Change of basis} --- the similarity transform $P^{-1}AP$ rewrites the same map in new coordinates; an eigenbasis makes it diagonal.
    \item \textbf{Eigentheory} --- $A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}$, the characteristic equation $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$, and the trace/determinant shortcut for $2\times 2$.
    \item \textbf{Eigenvalues through ODEs} --- $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ decouples $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ into scalar modes, the gateway to Unit 15.
\end{itemize}
\end{topicsbox}

\vspace{0.6em}

\setcounter{section}{14}

% ---- Module 14.1 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Vectors, Span, and Linear Transformations}
\emph{Linear combinations, span, and basis; linear transformations and the matrices that encode them; columns as images of the basis vectors; composition as matrix product.}

\begin{formulabox}[Linear Combination, Span, and Basis]
\[ a\mathbf{v} + b\mathbf{w} \ \ (a,b \text{ scalars}); \quad \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{v}_i\} = \Big\{\textstyle\sum_i c_i \mathbf{v}_i\Big\}; \quad \text{basis} \iff \text{independent } \textbf{and} \text{ spanning}. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Linearity Condition]
\[ T(a\mathbf{u} + b\mathbf{v}) = a\,T(\mathbf{u}) + b\,T(\mathbf{v}) \quad \text{for all scalars } a,b \text{ and vectors } \mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[The Matrix of a Transformation]
\[ A = \big[\, T(\mathbf{e}_1) \mid T(\mathbf{e}_2) \mid \cdots \,\big], \qquad T(\mathbf{x}) = A\mathbf{x}, \qquad (T_2 \circ T_1) \leftrightarrow A_2 A_1. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Build the Matrix of a Linear Map]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Apply $T$ to each standard basis vector $\mathbf{e}_j$.
    \item Record each image $T(\mathbf{e}_j)$ as the $j$-th \emph{column}.
    \item Assemble the columns into the matrix $A$.
    \item Read off the action as $T(\mathbf{x}) = A\mathbf{x}$ and verify on a test vector.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Apply a Matrix to a Vector]
Let $A$ have rows $(1,2)$ and $(3,4)$. Compute the image of $\mathbf{x}=(1,1)$ by dotting each row with $\mathbf{x}$.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{A\mathbf{x} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\text{row 1: } (1,2)\cdot(1,1) = 1+2 = 3} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\text{row 2: } (3,4)\cdot(1,1) = 3+4 = 7} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{A\mathbf{x} = (3,\,7)} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
A linear transformation is \textcolor{accentorange}{completely determined by where it sends the basis}. The columns of $A$ are exactly the images $T(\mathbf{e}_j)$, and composing two maps corresponds to \emph{multiplying} their matrices (right-to-left).
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
A map with a nonzero shift, $T(\mathbf{u}) = \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{c}$ with $\mathbf{c}\neq\mathbf{0}$, is \textcolor{warnred}{affine, not linear} --- it moves the origin. Linearity \emph{requires} $T(\mathbf{0})=\mathbf{0}$. Also, matrix multiplication is not commutative: $A_2A_1\neq A_1A_2$ in general, so composition order matters.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 14.2 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{The Determinant and Change of Basis}
\emph{The determinant as a signed area-scaling factor; the formula $ad-bc$; what zero and negative determinants mean; change-of-basis matrices and the similarity transform $P^{-1}AP$.}

\begin{formulabox}[The $2\times 2$ Determinant]
\[ \det\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix} = ad - bc \;=\; \text{signed factor by which the map scales area.} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Reading the Determinant Geometrically]
\[ \lvert\det A\rvert = \text{area scaling}, \quad \det A = 0 \Rightarrow \text{collapse (dependent columns)}, \quad \det A < 0 \Rightarrow \text{orientation reversed.} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Change of Basis and Similarity]
\[ B = P^{-1} A P, \qquad \text{columns of } P = \text{new basis vectors in the old coordinates}; \quad \text{eigenbasis} \Rightarrow B \text{ diagonal.} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Re-express a Map in a New Basis]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Place the new basis vectors (written in old coordinates) as the columns of $P$.
    \item Compute the inverse $P^{-1}$.
    \item Form the similarity transform $B = P^{-1} A P$.
    \item If the columns of $P$ are eigenvectors of $A$, then $B$ is diagonal with the eigenvalues on the diagonal.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Determinant and Its Meaning]
Compute the determinant of $A$ with rows $(3,1)$ and $(2,4)$, then interpret it geometrically.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\det A = ad - bc = (3)(4) - (1)(2)} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\det A = 12 - 2 = 10} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lvert\det A\rvert = 10 \Rightarrow \text{areas are scaled by a factor of } 10} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\det A > 0 \Rightarrow \text{orientation is preserved (no flip).}} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
The determinant packages two facts in one number: its \textcolor{accentorange}{magnitude is the area-scaling factor} and its \textcolor{accentorange}{sign records orientation}. Because a change of basis only relabels coordinates, \emph{similar} matrices $P^{-1}AP$ share the same determinant, trace, and eigenvalues.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
Do not confuse a \textcolor{warnred}{negative} determinant with a \textcolor{warnred}{zero} determinant. Negative still means invertible --- the plane is merely flipped. Only $\det A = 0$ collapses a dimension and makes $A$ singular (columns linearly dependent).
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 14.3 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors}
\emph{The eigenvalue equation $A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}$; the characteristic equation $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$; trace as the sum and determinant as the product of eigenvalues; the trace/determinant shortcut for $2\times 2$ matrices.}

\begin{formulabox}[The Eigenvalue Equation]
\[ A\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}, \quad \mathbf{v}\neq\mathbf{0}: \quad A \text{ only \emph{stretches} } \mathbf{v} \text{ along its own line, by the factor } \lambda. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[The Characteristic Equation]
\[ \det(A - \lambda I) = 0; \qquad 2\times 2:\ \ \lambda^2 - (\operatorname{tr}A)\,\lambda + \det A = 0. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Trace--Determinant Shortcut]
\[ \lambda = m \pm \sqrt{m^2 - p}, \quad m = \tfrac{1}{2}\operatorname{tr}A,\ \ p = \det A; \qquad \operatorname{tr}A = \lambda_1+\lambda_2,\ \ \det A = \lambda_1\lambda_2. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Find Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Form $A - \lambda I$.
    \item Set $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$ and solve for $\lambda$ (for $2\times 2$, use $\lambda^2-(\operatorname{tr}A)\lambda+\det A=0$).
    \item For each $\lambda$, solve $(A-\lambda I)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$ for the eigenvector $\mathbf{v}$.
    \item Verify $A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors of a Symmetric Matrix]
For $A$ with rows $(2,1)$ and $(1,2)$, use $\operatorname{tr}A=4$ and $\det A=3$, then find an eigenvector for each eigenvalue.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda = m \pm \sqrt{m^2-p} = 2 \pm \sqrt{4-3} = 2 \pm 1 \ \Rightarrow\ \lambda_1=3,\ \lambda_2=1} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda_1=3:\ (A-3I)\mathbf{v}=\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0} \ \Rightarrow\ \mathbf{v}_1=(1,1)} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda_2=1:\ (A-I)\mathbf{v}=\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0} \ \Rightarrow\ \mathbf{v}_2=(1,-1)} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\text{Check: } A(1,1)=(3,3)=3(1,1) \ \checkmark} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
Eigenvectors are the \textcolor{accentorange}{special directions a matrix leaves invariant} --- it only scales them, by the eigenvalue. In the eigenbasis the matrix becomes diagonal, which is exactly what makes powers, exponentials, and differential equations easy.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
The characteristic equation is $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$, \textcolor{warnred}{not} $\det A - \lambda = 0$, and the $2\times 2$ form carries a \emph{minus} sign on the trace: $\lambda^2-(\operatorname{tr}A)\lambda+\det A$. Handy check: for diagonal or triangular matrices the eigenvalues sit on the diagonal --- the off-diagonal entries do not change them.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 14.4 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Eigenvalues Through Differential Equations}
\emph{The trial solution $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ that reduces $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ to the eigenvalue problem; one exponential mode per eigenpair; the general solution by superposition; growth, decay, and oscillation from the sign and type of the eigenvalues.}

\begin{formulabox}[The Eigen-Ansatz]
\[ \text{Try } \mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}: \quad \mathbf{x}'=\lambda e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v} = A e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v} \ \Rightarrow\ A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[General Solution (Distinct Eigenvalues)]
\[ \mathbf{x}(t) = c_1 e^{\lambda_1 t}\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}\mathbf{v}_2 \quad (\text{superposition of the eigen-modes}). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Stability from the Eigenvalues]
\[ \lambda<0:\ \text{decay}, \quad \lambda>0:\ \text{growth}, \quad \lambda=\alpha\pm i\beta:\ \text{oscillation (decays if } \alpha<0\text{)}. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Solve $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ by Eigenvalues]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Find the eigenvalues of $A$ from $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$.
    \item Find an eigenvector $\mathbf{v}_i$ for each eigenvalue $\lambda_i$.
    \item Form the eigen-modes $e^{\lambda_i t}\mathbf{v}_i$.
    \item Superpose: $\mathbf{x}=\sum_i c_i e^{\lambda_i t}\mathbf{v}_i$, then fix the $c_i$ from the initial conditions.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Solve a Coupled System]
Solve $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ for $A$ with rows $(2,1)$ and $(1,2)$, reusing the eigenpairs $\lambda_1=3,\ \mathbf{v}_1=(1,1)$ and $\lambda_2=1,\ \mathbf{v}_2=(1,-1)$ from 14.3.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\text{Each eigenpair gives a mode: } e^{3t}(1,1) \text{ and } e^{t}(1,-1)} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\mathbf{x}(t) = c_1 e^{3t}\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\end{pmatrix} + c_2 e^{t}\begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\end{pmatrix}} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\text{Both } \lambda>0 \Rightarrow \text{every trajectory grows; the origin is an unstable node.}} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
The eigenvalue method \textcolor{accentorange}{decouples a coupled linear system into independent scalar modes}. Along each eigenvector $A$ acts as multiplication by $\lambda$, so $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ collapses to $x'=\lambda x$ with solution $e^{\lambda t}$ --- the foundation of every technique in Unit 15.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
In the superposition each exponential must ride its \textcolor{warnred}{own} eigenvector: $c_1 e^{\lambda_1 t}\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}\mathbf{v}_2$, never a crossed pairing. And long-term behavior is set by the sign of $\operatorname{Re}(\lambda)$ --- a single positive eigenvalue makes the whole system unstable.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

\clearpage
\phantomsection
\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Practice Problems}
\section*{Practice Problems}

Work the following ten problems in order. They climb through linear combinations and bases, the matrix of a transformation, the determinant and its geometric meaning, the similarity transform, the characteristic equation and the trace/determinant shortcut, eigenvectors, the eigen-ansatz, and a capstone that solves a coupled system $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ end to end. Solutions follow in Part III.

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=2em]
    \item \textbf{(Linear combination)} Compute the linear combination $2(1,0) + 3(0,1)$.

    \item \textbf{(Basis test)} State the two conditions a set must meet to be a basis, then decide whether $(1,0)$ and $(2,0)$ form a basis of $\mathbb{R}^2$.

    \item \textbf{(Matrix of a transformation)} For the matrix $A$ with rows $(1,2)$ and $(3,4)$, compute the image $A\mathbf{x}$ of $\mathbf{x}=(1,1)$.

    \item \textbf{(Determinant, compute)} Find the determinant of the matrix with rows $(3,1)$ and $(2,4)$, and state the factor by which it scales area.

    \item \textbf{(Determinant, meaning)} Compute the determinant of the matrix with rows $(1,2)$ and $(2,4)$. Is the matrix invertible? What does this say about its columns?

    \item \textbf{(Change of basis)} Write the formula that expresses a matrix $A$ in the basis given by a change-of-basis matrix $P$, and state what makes the result diagonal.

    \item \textbf{(Characteristic equation)} Write the $2\times 2$ characteristic equation in terms of the trace and determinant, then find the eigenvalues of the matrix with rows $(2,1)$ and $(1,2)$.

    \item \textbf{(Eigenvectors)} For that same matrix (rows $(2,1)$, $(1,2)$), find an eigenvector for each eigenvalue.

    \item \textbf{(Eigen-ansatz)} Show that substituting $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ into $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ produces the eigenvalue equation $A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}$.

    \item \textbf{(Capstone --- solve a system)} Using the eigenpairs from Problems 7--8, write the general solution of $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ for $A$ with rows $(2,1)$ and $(1,2)$, and classify the equilibrium at the origin.

\end{enumerate}

\clearpage
\phantomsection
\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Complete Solutions}
\section*{Complete Solutions}

\noindent\textbf{Solution 1.}\quad Scale each vector and add componentwise:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{2(1,0) + 3(0,1) = (2,0) + (0,3) = (2,3).} \]
The scalars stay attached to their own directions; they do not merge into a single axis.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 2.}\quad A basis must be \emph{linearly independent} and must \emph{span} the space. The vectors $(1,0)$ and $(2,0)$ are parallel, since $(2,0)=2(1,0)$, so they are dependent and span only the $x$-axis:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\operatorname{span}\{(1,0),(2,0)\} = \{(t,0)\} \neq \mathbb{R}^2.} \]
They are \textbf{not} a basis of $\mathbb{R}^2$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 3.}\quad Dot each row with the input $\mathbf{x}=(1,1)$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{A\mathbf{x} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1+2 \\ 3+4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix}.} \]

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 4.}\quad Use $\det = ad-bc$ with $a=3,b=1,c=2,d=4$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\det A = (3)(4) - (1)(2) = 12 - 2 = 10.} \]
The map scales every area by a factor of $\lvert\det A\rvert = 10$, and since $\det A>0$ it preserves orientation.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 5.}\quad Compute the determinant:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\det\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = (1)(4) - (2)(2) = 4 - 4 = 0.} \]
A zero determinant means the matrix is \textbf{not invertible}: it collapses the plane onto a line, so its columns are linearly dependent (indeed $(2,4)=2(1,2)$).

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 6.}\quad The similarity transform rewrites $A$ in the new coordinates:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{B = P^{-1} A P,} \]
where the columns of $P$ are the new basis vectors in the old coordinates. If those columns are eigenvectors of $A$, then $B$ is \textbf{diagonal}, with the eigenvalues on the diagonal.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 7.}\quad The characteristic equation is
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda^2 - (\operatorname{tr}A)\lambda + \det A = 0.} \]
For rows $(2,1),(1,2)$: $\operatorname{tr}A=4$, $\det A=3$, so
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 3 = (\lambda-3)(\lambda-1) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda = 3,\ 1.} \]

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 8.}\quad Solve $(A-\lambda I)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$ for each eigenvalue:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda=3:\ \begin{pmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0} \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_1=(1,1);} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda=1:\ \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0} \Rightarrow \mathbf{v}_2=(1,-1).} \]

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 9.}\quad Differentiate the trial $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ (with $\mathbf{v}$ constant) and substitute:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\mathbf{x}' = \lambda e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}, \qquad A\mathbf{x} = e^{\lambda t} A\mathbf{v}.} \]
Setting $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ and cancelling the nonzero scalar $e^{\lambda t}$ leaves
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda\mathbf{v} = A\mathbf{v},} \]
exactly the eigenvalue problem.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 10.}\quad Each eigenpair contributes one exponential mode; superpose them:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\mathbf{x}(t) = c_1 e^{3t}\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\end{pmatrix} + c_2 e^{t}\begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\end{pmatrix}.} \]
Both eigenvalues are real and positive, so every trajectory grows without bound: the origin is an \textbf{unstable node} (a source).

\medskip

\begin{conceptbox}[Unit Key Takeaway]
Every problem above is one geometric story: a matrix \emph{moves} space. Its \textcolor{accentorange}{columns} say where the basis lands, its \textcolor{accentorange}{determinant} says how area is scaled and whether orientation flips, and its \textcolor{accentorange}{eigenvectors} mark the directions it merely stretches. Feeding those eigenpairs into $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ turns a coupled system into independent exponential modes --- the exact machinery Unit 15 runs on.
\end{conceptbox}

\end{document}
